meta.purpose: Source of truth for all user-facing copy across marketing site, CLI, web client, and transactional communications. Every string lives here; surfaces consume by key. This enables consistent voice, easier iteration, and localization readiness.
meta.brandDecision: TryDogfooding. The throughline is that operators should eat their own cooking — build the software they wish existed, own it, scale it. The name does the positioning work.
meta.cliDecision: Primary command is `dogfood`; `trydogfooding` works as an alias. Short for daily use, full name preserved in installs and docs.
meta.voicePrinciple: Operator-first. Write like a thoughtful friend who happens to build software — direct, warm, specific, never condescending.

principles: Operator-first. Their problem, their language, their tools.
Direct. Lead with the outcome, not the process.
Specific. Real numbers, real names, real scenarios over abstract claims.
Warm. Contractions welcome. Second person. One sentence per paragraph is fine.
Honest. Never oversell. Surface limitations before users discover them.
Confident without hype. The product is good enough that it doesn't need adjectives.

toneByContext.marketing: confident, welcoming, specific to the reader's role
toneByContext.email: personal, as if from a trusted colleague

errorMessage.good: Your HubSpot connection expired. Let's reconnect — takes about 30 seconds.
errorMessage.bad: Error: OAuth token invalid. Please re-authenticate.
errorMessage.why: Good version names the system, tells user what happened, gives a time estimate, and starts the action.

marketingHero.good: Build the software you wish existed. Eat your own cooking.
marketingHero.bad: Revolutionary AI platform that empowers operators to unlock productivity.
marketingHero.why: Good version states the outcome and names the philosophy. Bad version is jargon soup.

cliPrompt.good: Which tools do you use day-to-day? We'll set up connections for them.
cliPrompt.bad: Please select your desired integrations from the following list:
cliPrompt.why: Good version asks a question in plain language. Bad version is bureaucratic.

brand.name: TryDogfooding
brand.tagline: Build the software you wish existed.
brand.taglineLong: Build the software you wish existed. Eat your own cooking.
brand.elevatorPitch: TryDogfooding is a starter kit for operators — in CX, GTM, marketing, and back office — who want to build their own software instead of waiting for engineering. Open source tool, cohort course, real outcomes.
brand.philosophy: Every company ships better software when the people closest to the problem can build for themselves. Dogfooding is not a nice-to-have — it's how craft happens. TryDogfooding gives operators the starter kit and the skills to do it.
brand.whatDogfoodingMeans: Dogfooding is the practice of using what you build. It's what separates teams that ship software that works from teams that ship software that looks like it works. The best operators already know their workflows intimately. Dogfooding means turning that knowledge into software they use — and own.

global.tagline: Build the software you wish existed.
global.shortDescription: TryDogfooding gives operators — in CX, GTM, marketing, and back office — a starter kit to build their own software using Claude Code. Open source tool, cohort course, real outcomes.

hero.eyebrow: For operators. By someone who's done the work.
hero.headline: Build the software you wish existed.
hero.subhead: You already run the workflows. You already know the tools. You already know your data. TryDogfooding plus Claude Code is the missing layer — a starter kit that turns your documented process into personal software that runs reliably.
hero.trustNote: Open source. AGPLv3 licensed. Your data stays yours.

whyDogfoodingBanner.heading: Why we call it dogfooding
whyDogfoodingBanner.body: The best software comes from people who use what they build. Support leads know support. GTM leads know GTM. Marketers know marketing. You've been an expert operator for years. Now you get to be the developer, too — for your own workflows, your own way.

personaStrip.heading: Which operator are you?
personaStrip.subhead: Pick the one closest to your role. We'll show you what operators like you have built.

personas.label: GTM
personas.description: Research accounts, draft outreach in your voice, surface the meetings that matter.

personas.label: Customer experience
personas.description: Triage tickets, draft responses in team voice, escalate the right ones.

personas.label: Marketing
personas.description: Generate variations, score them against past winners, ship faster.

personas.label: Back office
personas.description: Reconcile, verify, and document the repetitive parts of your procedures.

howItWorks.heading: How it works
howItWorks.subhead: Four steps. No installing Node by hand. No reading AI theory. No becoming a developer.

steps.title: Set up your workspace

steps.description: See your own workflow in your own voice, with your own tools, producing real results.

steps.title: Scale when you're ready
steps.description: Run on schedule. Share with your team. Eventually productize it. Same tool, every stage.

socialProof.heading: Built by someone who's done the work
socialProof.description: TryDogfooding's creator shipped backend systems at HashiCorp (including the Nomad UI), taught 300+ students a year at Parsons School of Design, and has spent years teaching operators how to build. TryDogfooding is the tool that came out of that teaching — the thing that actually makes 'build your own software' real for non-developers.

caseStudyTeaser.heading: Real operators. Real outcomes.
caseStudyTeaser.subhead: Every case study names the person, their stack, and the specific time they got back. No composites. No anonymous 'customers.'

hero.headline: Eat your own cooking.
hero.subhead: A short manifesto on why operators should build their own software.

body.heading: Who builds software today
body.body: Software today is built by developers, for users. The developer is rarely the person closest to the problem. The user is rarely the person building the tool. This gap is why software so often misses — it's assembled from assumptions instead of lived experience.

body.heading: Who should build software
body.body: The best software comes from people who use what they build. This is what 'dogfooding' means in engineering culture — if the team doesn't use their own product, why should anyone else? But operators — the people running support, GTM, marketing, ops — have never been able to dogfood their own workflows. They describe, they request, they wait.

body.heading: What changed
body.body: AI doesn't replace developers. It collapses the distance between thinking and making. An operator who knows exactly what their workflow should do — because they've been doing it for years — can now build software that does it. Not a developer's interpretation. Not a no-code approximation. The actual thing.

body.heading: What TryDogfooding is
body.body: A starter kit for operators who want to build their own software. An open-source CLI that sets up Claude Code and connects your tools. Templates for the workflows you already do. A four-week cohort that takes you from zero to three running workflows. A community of operators doing the same thing.

body.heading: What you'll need
body.body: A laptop. A willingness to type commands. A specific workflow you'd rather not do by hand anymore. That's it.

body.heading: What you'll get
body.body: Software you own. In your voice. Following your rules. Running against your real data. That you built.

hero.headline: Your GTM stack, your voice, without the HITL tax.
hero.subhead: You already know what good outreach looks like. You've written the playbooks. The problem isn't knowing — it's scaling your attention. TryDogfooding lets you build an assistant that researches accounts, drafts sequences in your voice, and flags the ones that need you personally.

scenario.heading: A Tuesday that actually works
scenario.body: 8:00 AM. You open Slack. Your workflow ran overnight: 40 accounts researched from your target list, each with a one-paragraph brief, a draft email in your voice, and a flag if it thinks you should call instead. You review 40 drafts in 30 minutes — approving most, rewriting five, killing two. Sent. Time to focus on the demos where your attention actually compounds.

stackSupported.heading: Works with the tools you already use
stackSupported.note: Don't see your stack? Tell us — integrations ship monthly.

templates.name: Research an account
templates.description: One URL or company name in, a full briefing out.

templates.name: Draft outreach sequence
templates.description: Target account plus your voice sample in, three-email sequence out.

templates.name: Weekly pipeline review
templates.description: Gong calls and HubSpot activity in, priority list for the week out.

templates.name: Call prep
templates.description: Meeting on calendar plus attendee context in, briefing note out.

templates.name: Follow-up triage
templates.description: Recent calls in, who to follow up with and how, out.

items.question: Won't this sound like AI slop?
items.answer: Only if you feed it AI-slop examples. The workflow runs on your voice samples, your past winning emails, your playbooks. If your outreach already sounds good, so will this.

items.question: What if it makes up facts about an account?
items.answer: Every output includes sources. You see exactly which Gong call, which HubSpot field, which article it drew from. Hallucinations get caught in review, not in production.

items.question: Do I need to learn prompt engineering?
items.answer: Not as a discipline. You'll learn to describe what you want in plain English, which is a skill you already have. The workshop covers this in 20 minutes.

cta.heading: Come to the free workshop
cta.body: 60 minutes. Live. You'll build one working GTM workflow against your actual HubSpot or Attio data.

hero.headline: Triage in 30 minutes instead of 4 hours. In your team's voice.
hero.subhead: Your team has a way of talking to customers. Your past tickets show what works. Your escalation rules live in Notion. TryDogfooding lets you build an assistant that reads all of that and drafts responses you'll actually send.

scenario.heading: Monday morning, before standup
scenario.body: You run one command. Overnight tickets get categorized. Responses drafted in your team's voice, citing your help docs. Three are flagged as needing a human touch — real complaints, edge cases, VIP customers. You review drafts in 30 minutes, send what's good, rewrite the rest. Standup starts. You actually have time for it.

stackSupported.heading: Works with the tools your support team lives in

templates.name: Triage new tickets
templates.description: Categorize, draft in team voice, flag escalations.

templates.name: Draft response from help docs
templates.description: Ticket plus your Notion help library, out comes a sourced draft.

templates.name: Weekly theme report
templates.description: Last week's tickets in, themes and trend callouts out for Monday standup.

templates.name: VIP watch
templates.description: Flag tickets from named accounts or high-value segments.

templates.name: Escalation pre-check
templates.description: Ticket plus team rules, out comes a recommendation on who handles it.

items.question: Will it send bad responses without review?
items.answer: Not by default. Every draft goes to a queue you review. You can choose to auto-send for the highest-confidence categories later, once you trust it.

items.question: What about customer data privacy?
items.answer: Customer data never leaves the tools you already use, except for the specific call to Claude Code — which follows Anthropic's data policy. We document exactly what flows where.

items.question: How is this different from Zendesk's AI?
items.answer: Zendesk's AI is generic. This is your playbook, your voice, your escalation rules. You own it. You can move it to another ticketing system if you ever switch.

cta.heading: Try it with your actual tickets
cta.body: Live workshop, 60 minutes. You'll build a triage workflow on your real Zendesk or Intercom instance.

hero.headline: Fifty ad variations in your brand voice. Before lunch.
hero.subhead: Creative iteration is volumetric. You need variations, hooks, angles. You also need them to sound like your brand and perform like your past winners. TryDogfooding builds the pipeline.

scenario.heading: Campaign launch day
scenario.body: You give your workflow a brief: product, angle, audience. It pulls your past winning ads from Meta Ads, your brand voice guide from Notion, your content library. Back come 50 variations — copy, headlines, hooks — each scored against what's historically worked. You pick 20 to test. You push them to Meta in one move. You ship before lunch.

templates.name: Content repurposing

templates.name: Weekly performance digest
templates.description: Meta and Google data, out comes what worked and what to try.

templates.name: Brief-to-asset pipeline

cta.heading: Ship your first variation pipeline

hero.headline: The repetitive parts of your documented process. Done reliably.
hero.subhead: You have the procedures written down. You know exactly what needs to happen. What you don't have is time to do it twice. TryDogfooding turns your SOPs into software that follows them — with the audit trail your finance team needs.

scenario.heading: Month-end close, without the weekend
scenario.body: Your reconciliation workflow runs on schedule. Invoices from Bill.com matched to entries in NetSuite. Discrepancies flagged with context. Everything logged with timestamps and reasoning for the audit trail. You review exceptions instead of doing reconciliation by hand.

items.q: Do I need to know how to code?
items.a: No. If you can follow step-by-step instructions and describe what you want in plain English, you can do this.

items.q: What does it cost to run workflows after the workshop?
items.a: The CLI is free and open source. Claude Code has its own pricing from Anthropic. Most operators spend $10-50/month on API costs for regular use. We cover pricing transparently in the workshop.

items.q: Will this work with my specific tools?
items.a: We support GTM, CX, Marketing, and Back-office stacks. See the persona pages for your specific tool list. If we don't yet, tell us — we add integrations monthly.
Yes. Everyone who signs up gets the recording, even if you can't attend live.

hero.headline: The Dogfooding Cohort
hero.subhead: Four weeks. You ship three workflows that save you measurable time every week. Small group, live sessions, peer community, direct instructor access.

yes.heading: Yes if you're:

Comfortable in your SaaS stack — you run the tools, you're not scared of them

Looking to resell or productize immediately (that's a later course)
Looking for a fully-managed done-for-you service
Not sure what problem you'd solve (come to the free workshop first)

weeks.title: Your second and third workflows
weeks.body: Apply the pattern to two more problems in your role. By end of week 3 you have three workflows saving time weekly.

weeks.title: Making it reliable
weeks.body: Scheduling, error recovery, cost management. Your workflows become reliable software, not experiments.

enrollmentCta.heading: Next cohort starts soon

items.q: What happens if I fall behind in a week?
items.a: Sessions are recorded. Office hours are available to catch up. Nobody finishes without shipping — that's the commitment.

items.q: Will this work for my specific role?
items.a: The cohort covers GTM, CX, marketing, and back-office. If you're outside those, email us before enrolling so we can make sure it's a fit.

items.q: Can my company pay for this?
items.a: Yes. We provide invoices. Many students expense it as professional development.
Two 60-minute live sessions plus 3-5 hours of building on your own. More if you want to build faster.
Teams of 3+ get a discounted rate. Email team@trydogfooding.com for details.

hero.headline: Built by someone who's done the work.
hero.subhead: TryDogfooding isn't a theory. It's the tool we wished existed when teaching operators to build software.

creatorBio.heading: About the creator
creatorBio.body: Jai Malik is a senior software engineer and educator. He shipped backend systems at HashiCorp (including the Nomad UI), taught 300+ students a year at Parsons School of Design, and has spent the last several years teaching operators how to build software. He holds a Series 65 and trains in boxing — neither of which is relevant to TryDogfooding, but both of which tell you something about how he approaches craft.

philosophy.heading: Why we built this
philosophy.body: Operators have always been the unsung infrastructure of every successful company. They know the workflows. They know the tools. They know the data. What they haven't had is a way to turn that knowledge into software they own. AI finally makes that possible. TryDogfooding is the starter kit that makes it practical.

openSource.heading: Open source commitment
openSource.body: The CLI is AGPLv3 licensed. Forever. This means anyone who modifies and runs it as a network service must share their changes — but you can use it freely on your own machine. We monetize through the cohort course and (eventually) a hosted runner for teams. The tool itself stays free and yours.

quickstart.heading: Quickstart
quickstart.intro: If you're coming from the free workshop, you've already done the setup. This page exists for anyone arriving fresh.

steps.heading: 1. Install
steps.body: On Mac or Linux, run: `curl -sSL get.trydogfooding.com | sh` — this installs Node if needed, then TryDogfooding itself. Takes about 2 minutes.
steps.note: Windows users: we recommend WSL for now. Native Windows support is on the roadmap.

steps.heading: 2. Set up your workspace
steps.body: Run `dogfood init`. You'll be asked which stack you work with (GTM, CX, Marketing, Back-office) and asked to sign into your tools. Takes about 15 minutes depending on how many integrations you set up.

steps.heading: 3. Run your first workflow
steps.body: Your init finishes with a welcome tour. Follow the prompt to run it. You'll see your real data come back through your new workflow.

steps.heading: 4. Customize or add more
steps.body: Use `dogfood workflow list` to see what's available. Use `dogfood workflow new` to add more templates. Edit the `CLAUDE.md` in any workflow folder to teach it your voice.

404.heading: This page doesn't exist.
404.body: The link might be stale, or we might have moved it. Here's where you might want to go instead.

500.heading: Something's wrong on our end.
500.body: We're investigating. Try again in a minute, or check our status page.

maintenance.heading: Brief maintenance in progress.
maintenance.body: We'll be back shortly. Your CLI workflows are unaffected — this only affects the web surfaces.

global.helpHeader: dogfood — build the software you wish existed
global.aliasNote: You can type `trydogfooding` or `dogfood` — they're the same command.

welcome.body: Let's get your workspace set up. This takes about 15 minutes.
welcome.ready: Ready to start? (Press Enter, or Ctrl+C to quit)

notFound.message: We didn't find Claude Code on your system.
notFound.explanation: Claude Code is the engine that runs your workflows. It's made by Anthropic, separate from TryDogfooding.

claudeCodeCheck.installing: Installing Claude Code... this takes about a minute.

installFailed.whatToDo: Try installing manually: https://docs.claude.com/claude-code/install — then run `dogfood init` again.
installFailed.support: Stuck? Ask in the community: https://trydogfooding.com/community

claudeCodeCheck.alreadyInstalled: Claude Code is already installed. Skipping.

stackChoice.prompt: Which kind of work do you do most day-to-day?

options.label: GTM — research accounts, outreach, sales ops
Something else — I'll pick integrations manually

stackChoice.hint: You can change this later or have multiple stacks.

integrationsPreview.header: We'll set up connections for your {stack} stack:

integrationAuth.browserOpening: Opening your browser to sign into {integration}.
integrationAuth.browserFallback: If the browser didn't open, visit: {url}
integrationAuth.waitingForAuth: Waiting for you to sign in...

options: [s] Skip for now (you can fix this later with `dogfood integrations fix {integration}`)

created: Initialized git repo for change history

verification.starting: Running a verification workflow to make sure everything works...
verification.success: Verification passed. Your setup works end to end.

failed.body: Something is off, but your setup isn't broken — we couldn't confirm it works. Run `dogfood doctor` for a detailed check.

nextSteps: Try your first workflow: `dogfood run welcome-tour`
See what else is available: `dogfood workflow list`

node.fail: Node not found or too old (need 20+)

claudeCode.fail: Claude Code not found or not working

summary.canFix: Run `dogfood doctor --fix` to try auto-repair where possible.

list.empty: No workflows yet. Run `dogfood workflow new` to add one.

new.prompt: Which template would you like to start from?

afterSelection.namePrompt: What would you like to call it? (default: {templateName})
afterSelection.customize: Let's customize it for you. A few quick questions...

nextSteps: Or edit the CLAUDE.md to teach it your voice first: {editCommand}

budgetExceeded.message: This run hit the budget cap at ${budget}.
budgetExceeded.whatToDo: Raise the cap in the workflow config, or split the work into smaller runs.

timeout.whatToDo: The workflow might be stuck in a loop or the task is too large. Check the log: `dogfood history show {runId}`

authExpired.whatToDo: [r] Reconnect and resume, [q] Quit and fix later

add.prompt: Which integration would you like to add?
add.success: {integration} connected. You can now use it in workflows.

stillBroken.whatToDo: This might be a provider outage. Check their status page, or ask in the community.

networkOffline.heading: You're offline.
networkOffline.body: TryDogfooding needs a connection to Claude Code and your integrations.
networkOffline.whatToDo: Check your connection and try again.

anthropicRateLimit.heading: Rate limited by Anthropic.
anthropicRateLimit.body: Too many requests at once. This usually clears in a minute.

diskSpace.heading: Not enough disk space.
diskSpace.body: Need at least {needed} free; you have {available}.
diskSpace.whatToDo: Free up some space and try again.

unexpected.heading: Something unexpected happened.
unexpected.body: This is probably a bug on our end.
unexpected.whatToDo: Run `dogfood doctor` for diagnostics, or report this: https://github.com/trydogfooding/cli/issues — include the log at ~/.dogfood/logs/latest.log

signIn.heading: Sign in to TryDogfooding
signIn.subhead: We'll email you a magic link. No passwords to remember.
signIn.successMessage: Check your email for a link to sign in. Link expires in 15 minutes.
signIn.errorMessage: We couldn't send that email. Check the address and try again.

linkExpired.heading: This sign-in link expired.

linkInvalid.heading: This link isn't valid.
linkInvalid.body: It might have been used already, or been changed by your email client.

fromAddress.name: TryDogfooding

confirmation.subject: You're signed up for the workshop
confirmation.preheader: Here's what to expect and how to prepare.
confirmation.body:
Hi {firstName},

You're in. We're excited to have you.

**When**: {date} at {time} ({timezone})
**Where**: {venue}
**How long**: 60 minutes

**Before you arrive**, please do this 10-minute prep so we can hit the ground running:

1. Make sure you have a laptop (any OS)
2. Have credentials ready for one tool you use daily — we'll connect it during the session
3. Think about one repetitive task from your week you'd like to try to automate

Anything else? Reply to this email. We read every one.

— Jai at TryDogfooding

Workshop is tomorrow at {time} ({timezone}). {joinInstructions}

Two quick things:

1. Bring credentials for one tool you want to connect
2. Bring a repetitive task you'd like to try to automate

See you tomorrow.

— Jai

postWorkshop.subject: Your workspace — what's next
postWorkshop.preheader: Recording, resources, and the clear path if you want to go deeper.

welcomeSequence.subject: Welcome to the Dogfooding Cohort
welcomeSequence.preheader: First steps before we kick off.
welcomeSequence.body:
Hi {firstName},

Welcome. I'm genuinely glad you're here.

Before our first session on {startDate}, please do these three things:

1. **Join the cohort community**: {communityLink}
2. **Complete the pre-work** (about 30 minutes): {preworkLink}
3. **Introduce yourself** in the cohort channel — your role, your stack, and one thing you want to have built by week 4

First live session: {firstSessionDate} at {time} ({timezone}).

Office hours every {officeHoursDay}. Show up if you're stuck. Show up if you're not.

— Jai

welcomeSequence.subject: Week 1 prep: your first workflow
welcomeSequence.body:
Hi {firstName},

First session is in {daysUntil} days. Here's what to have ready:

1. Your workspace set up ({quickstartLink})
2. One tool you want to connect first
3. One repetitive task from your week — the specific one we'll automate live

If any of this isn't clicking, come to office hours or ask in the channel. The whole point is nobody gets stuck alone.

— Jai

weekRecap.subject: Week {weekNumber} recap — {theme}

cohortComplete.subject: You finished the Dogfooding Cohort
cohortComplete.body:
Hi {firstName},

You did it. Four weeks, three workflows, real outcomes.

A few things now:

1. **Your case study**: If you're willing, we'd love to publish your story. Reply and I'll send the template. No pressure either way.
2. **The community stays open to you** — this isn't a goodbye.
3. **If you're ready to scale what you built**: the Team cohort opens {teamCohortDate}. Details: {teamCohortLink}
4. **If you want to productize your workflow**: That's a different kind of conversation. Reply and let's talk.

Seriously, congratulations. This work is hard and you did it.

— Jai

integrationExpired.subject: Reconnect {integration} — takes 30 seconds
integrationExpired.body:
Hi {firstName},

Your {integration} connection expired. Your {workflowName} workflow tried to run at {time} and couldn't complete.

**Fix it now**: {reconnectLink} (takes about 30 seconds)

Once reconnected, we'll offer to rerun the failed workflow.

— TryDogfooding

runFailed.subject: {workflowName} failed — what happened

budgetWarning50.subject: You're at 50% of your monthly budget

budgetWarning80.subject: You're at 80% of your monthly budget

budgetReached.subject: Budget cap reached — workflows paused
budgetReached.body:
Hi {firstName},

You've hit your ${cap} monthly cap, so we've paused workflow runs.

**To resume now**: Raise your cap at {settingsLink}
**Otherwise**: Runs resume automatically on the 1st of next month.

— TryDogfooding

weeklyDigest.subject: Your week on TryDogfooding: {summary}

platformOutage.subject: TryDogfooding is experiencing issues

resolved.subject: TryDogfooding issue resolved
resolved.body:
Hi {firstName},

The issue affecting {affectedSurface} is resolved as of {resolvedTime}.

If you experienced any failed runs during this period, they'll show in your run history. You can retry them anytime.

**Postmortem**: {postmortemLink} (published within 30 days)

Sorry for the trouble.

— TryDogfooding team

altTextGuidelines.principle: Every image has alt text. Decorative images use empty alt. Informative images describe the information, not the appearance.

examples.good: A diagram showing workflow execution: CLI command triggers Claude Code, which calls MCP servers for data, then writes output to Slack.

ariaLabelGuidelines.buttonsWithoutText: Always include aria-label for icon-only buttons

homepage.title: TryDogfooding — build the software you wish existed
homepage.description: Open source starter kit and cohort course for operators (GTM, CX, marketing, back office) to build their own software using Claude Code. Ship workflows that save hours a week.
homepage.keywords: claude code, personal software, ai workflows, operator tools, gtm automation, cx automation, marketing automation, dogfooding

workshopPage.title: Free workshop — your first working workflow | TryDogfooding
workshopPage.description: 60-minute live workshop. Build one working workflow against your real tools and data. Nothing to install beforehand.

cohortPage.title: The Dogfooding Cohort | TryDogfooding
cohortPage.description: Four-week cohort. Ship three workflows saving measurable time. Live sessions, small group, ongoing community. $497.

manifestoPage.title: Eat your own cooking — the TryDogfooding manifesto
manifestoPage.description: A short manifesto on why operators should build their own software, and what changed with AI.

changesFromV01: CLI primary command is now 'dogfood' (with 'trydogfooding' as alias)
Removed all references to Chai With Jai, Cash Is Clay, and Prompt Clinic as primary framing
Creator credentials (HashiCorp, Parsons) retained as trust signals, not ecosystem framing
Added Manifesto page that codifies the dogfooding throughline
Added 'Why we call it dogfooding' banner to homepage
Renamed 'Personal Software Revolution' cohort to 'The Dogfooding Cohort' — sharper, on-brand
openReviewQuestions: Cohort price point ($497) and name ('The Dogfooding Cohort') should be validated with two external target operators before launch.
Workshop preheader text optimized for inbox preview — A/B test candidates after first cohort.
Case study page currently has only template; needs real case studies from first cohort before publishing.
Voice guidelines should be tested: give a fresh writer this file and ask them to write new copy — does it match? If not, guidelines need sharpening.
The manifesto is a strong positioning artifact but also a commitment — every product decision should be auditable against it. Worth reviewing it quarterly.
CLI command 'dogfood' has some humor in it (literal meaning). Operator reactions should be sanity-checked — is it charming or off-putting?
nextActions: Review with one external target operator (GTM persona recommended) for naturalness of voice and reaction to the 'dogfood' command name.
Audit every string against forbiddenWords list before launch.
Secure trydogfooding.com domain, @trydogfooding handles across social, and the GitHub org.
Write three real case studies from first cohort before opening second cohort.
Design the manifesto page as a stand-alone visual artifact — it's the conceptual anchor and deserves the design treatment.
Pressure-test email subject lines against inbox competition before broader launch.
Add localization scaffolding (i18n keys) once English copy is stable.
